cycles.name: Foundation Forge
focuses: Build baseline movement quality across squat, split squat, and hinge patterns.
Begin shoulder stabilisation work and establish core anti-rotation capacity.

cycles.name: Structural Strength
focuses: Tempo-based strength in squat and RDL with shoulder-friendly pressing.
Deepen shoulder rehab integration and introduce rotational power for padel transfer.

paragraphs: Cycle 1 built the platform. This cycle is where we start loading it. You'll see the same patterns as last month — squat, hinge, press, pull — but now under controlled tempo with real intent.
Mentally, focus on owning the eccentric on every rep and keeping shoulder rehab as important as the strength work, not second to it. Earn the tempo. The weight will follow.
outcomes: Noticeably stronger Back Squat and Barbell RDL under tempo
Shoulder handles pressing without irritation or pinching
First exposure to real rotational power through med ball work
Cleaner landing mechanics on single-leg plyometrics
Improved anti-rotation core control under load

cycles.name: Rotational Engine
focuses: Convert strength into padel-specific rotational power through progressive med ball and cable work.
Sharpen reactive landing mechanics and lateral court movement.

teaser.subtitle: You've pressed, pulled, and squatted under control. Now we turn strength into speed.
paragraphs: Cycle 3 is where the med ball stops being a warm-up and becomes the point of the session.
The court will feel different after this cycle — your shots will have a weight behind them you haven't felt before.
Earn it in these four weeks.

cycles.name: Durability Build
focuses: Bulletproof the shoulder and knees through eccentric overload and tendon-focused loading.
Integrate sport-load management — training that coexists with harder padel weeks.

cycles.name: Integrated Athlete
focuses: Fuse strength, power, and conditioning into one robust athletic profile.
Establish an autoregulated maintenance rhythm for long-term use.

days.completionMessage: Day 1 complete — recover well and hydrate.

good: Steady pace — raise the heart rate gradually
bad: Don't sprint — this is preparation, not training

good: Keep both hips grounded
bad: Don't arch the lower back to rotate further

good: Chest tall, slow controlled descent
bad: Don't let the heels lift off the floor

good: Drive the knee forward over the toes
bad: Don't let the heel come off the ground

good: Soft knees on landing, neutral spine
Reset fully between reps
bad: Don't let knees cave inward on landing
Don't rush the next rep without resetting

good: Hold landing for 2 seconds
Control before you repeat
bad: Don't wobble out of the stick position
Don't collapse the landing knee inward

good: 3 sec down, 1 sec pause, 1 sec up
Push the floor away on the way up
bad: Don't rush the descent
Don't let knees cave in at the bottom

good: Drive through the heel
Squeeze glute at the top for a full second
bad: Don't push off the toes
Don't arch the lower back to get higher

good: Slow and controlled descent
Watch the standing knee in the mirror
bad: Don't let the standing knee cave inward
Don't drop quickly — control the eccentric

good: Dynamic press — extend fully and return
Resist rotation throughout the entire set
bad: Don't let the cable pull you into rotation
Don't hold your breath — breathe through each press

days.completionMessage: Day 2 complete — protect your shoulder, sleep well.

exercises.name: Arm Bike / Ski Erg / Cross Trainer

good: Moderate pace to warm the shoulder joint
bad: Don't push pace if the shoulder feels tight — stay easy

good: Lead with the shoulder blades, not the hands
bad: Don't shrug the traps toward the ears

good: Keep hands and elbows in contact with the wall
bad: Don't let the lower back arch off the wall

good: Elbow pinned to the ribs throughout
bad: Don't let the elbow drift away from the body

good: Slow and deliberate — this should never cause pain
bad: Don't use a weight that makes you cheat with the trunk

good: First exercise — prime the cuff before pressing
Light load, full range, lead with the elbows
bad: Don't go heavy — this is a quality movement
Don't let the elbows drop below shoulder height

good: Ribs stacked over hips — tall posture
Control the lockout, don't snap it
bad: Don't flare the ribs forward to press heavier
Stop the set if you feel a front-shoulder pinch

good: 1-second hold at the top of each rep
Squeeze the scapula inward at the peak
bad: Don't rotate the trunk to lift heavier
Don't let the shoulder round forward on the descent

good: Scapulae retracted into the bench throughout
Stop immediately if a pinch shows up at the front of the shoulder
bad: Don't flare the elbows to 90° — tuck them ~45°
Don't chase load this cycle — the shoulder comes first

good: Lead with the elbows driving down
Slight chest lift as the bar approaches
bad: Don't use the trunk to swing the weight down
Don't let the bar yank you on the way up

good: Very light or bodyweight — quality over load
Thumbs up on the Y, pinkies up on the T
bad: Don't shrug the traps to lift higher
Don't rush the reps — slow and deliberate

good: One arm only — resist the lean
Tall posture and even steps throughout
bad: Don't let the loaded side pull you sideways
Don't hold your breath — breathe normally while walking

days.completionMessage: Day 3 complete — the rotational work pays off on court.

good: Steady pace — raise heart rate gradually
bad: Don't sprint — this is preparation, not training

good: Step long, drop the back knee softly
bad: Don't let the front knee drive past the toes aggressively

good: Keep hips level — drive the back leg up, not out
bad: Don't let the standing-side hip drop

good: Press the lower back into the floor throughout
bad: Don't let the ribs flare as the leg extends

good: Minimal ground contact — think fast feet
Stiff ankles on landing
bad: Don't sink into a deep squat on landing
Don't chase jump height — chase contact speed

good: 2-second landing hold
Control the single-leg position before repeating
bad: Don't let the landing knee collapse inward
Don't rush the next rep — nail the stick first

good: Hips lead, trunk follows, arm releases last
Same sequence as your padel groundstrokes
bad: Don't go heavier — speed of rotation is the point
Don't throw arm-only — the power comes from the hips

good: Push hips back — don't just bend forward
Bar stays close to the legs throughout
bad: Don't round the lower back at the bottom
Don't squat the movement — this is a hinge

good: Hips level — don't open to the side
Slight bend in the standing knee
bad: Don't chase load — quality over weight
Don't rotate the torso as you hinge

good: Controlled tempo both directions
Trunk rotates — hips stay square
bad: Don't pull with the arms — drive through the trunk
Don't let the hips rotate with the torso

good: Hips stay still — resist the rotation
Slow and deliberate taps
bad: Don't let the hips shift side to side
Don't rush — speed defeats the purpose

cards.title: Your Shoulder Rule This Cycle
cards.body: Your shoulder is mostly pain-free now — we're loading it carefully but honestly. Posterior soreness after pressing is expected and productive. A sharp or pinching sensation at the front of the shoulder during Incline DB Press or Half-Kneeling DB Press is not. If you feel it, stop the set, drop the weight one notch, and flag it. The Side-Lying ER, Band ER, and Face Pull are doing the quiet heavy lifting here — treat them with the same focus as the squat.

cards.title: Why Tempo Matters More Than Weight Right Now
cards.body: You're still under a year into consistent lifting, so your tendons and joints are catching up to what your muscles can produce. The 3-1-1 tempos on Back Squat, RDL, and 3-Point Row aren't there to make sessions harder — they're there to build the connective tissue resilience that protects your shoulder and knees long-term. Slow the eccentric down like you mean it. If you can't hold the tempo, the weight is too heavy.

cards.title: The Med Ball Is the New Piece
cards.body: Last cycle had no rotational power work — this one does. The Rotational Wall Slams on Day 3 are a direct transfer to your padel groundstrokes. Stay at 2–3 kg: the stimulus is speed of rotation, not load. Hips lead, trunk follows, arm releases last — exactly like a clean forehand. Do not go heavier until the pattern feels automatic. Heavier ball means slower rotation and worse transfer.

cards.title: Day 2 Order Matters
cards.body: Cable Face Pull is marked Priority and placed first for a reason: it primes the cuff before any pressing load goes through the shoulder. Don't skip it or rush it to get to the 'real' exercises — on Day 2, the rehab and activation block is the real exercise. The pressing is only safe because the prep was honest.

cards.title: Padel on Off Days
cards.body: Keep padel moderate in weeks 1–3 — technical play, not maximum-effort matches. The rotational med ball work on Day 3 is already giving your shoulder and trunk a sport-specific stimulus, and stacking hard padel on top will slow the shoulder adaptation, not speed it up. Week 4 is a deload in the gym, so that's your window to push on court if you feel good.

cards.title: Recovery Is Where You Adapt
cards.body: You'll feel this cycle in new places — posterior shoulder, adductors, hamstrings from the tempo RDL. Sleep is non-negotiable: 7–9 hours, and protect the night before Day 2 especially. Tendon adaptation and rotator cuff remodelling both happen in deep sleep. Under-sleep this cycle and the shoulder progress stalls first.

cards.title: Week 4 Is a Deload — Not a Missed Week
cards.body: In week 4, volume drops by about 40%, plyos come out, and loads stay moderate. Same exercises, fewer sets. This is not you 'losing progress' — it's when your body consolidates everything you built in weeks 1–3. As a beginner you'll be tempted to skip it or push through. Don't. The deload is what makes Cycle 3 possible.

cards.title: Track Two Things Every Session
cards.body: Log the weight and RPE (1–10) for Back Squat, Barbell RDL, and whichever press you ran that day. That's it — don't overcomplicate it. This gives us clean signal for progressing loads into Cycle 3 and flags early if the shoulder is starting to creep in RPE (a sign of fatigue or irritation before pain shows up).
